sentences.text: Good morning.
Jack, J-A-C-K, Sandbrand, S-A-N-D-B-R-A-N-D.
Jacob.
Yeah.
I really don't know how to spell it in Hebrew.
Jacob, J-A-C-O-B.
That's--
Kolomyja, Poland.
K-O-L-O-M-Y-J-A.
In Poland-- it was Poland, up to 1939, between World War I and World War II.
It was a part of the Austria-Hungarian empire under Kaiser Franz Joseph.
One.
Yes.
'39, The Soviet army came in.
And it became part of the Soviet Union.
It was an occupied territory.
Well, they claimed that it was liberated territory.
[LAUGHS]
I had a two-year-old brother.
Two years older, yes.
Nathan.
No.
Our father's name was Ephraim Zalman Zeichner.
Right.
My mother's name was Mena Hacker Sandbrand.
Her father's name.
Yes.
Sandbrand was her mother's name.
Well, as I was given to-- it was explained to me-- is that the government in 1925, the local people did not recognize my mother's marriage to my father, because they were married only by a rabbi.
They had a ketubah.
But they did not have a civil ceremony at city hall.
So the marriage was considered by the Polish anti-Semitic government to be illegitimate.
And when my father passed away and I was supposed to be registered for public school, the registration office would not accept my father's name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for, for me.
And I had to register under my mother's maiden name.
Is, is that why your mother also used her family name instead of Hacker?
Exactly.
This situation lasted for many, many years in that part of Galicia.
Kolomyja, yeah.
Yes.
His name was Nathan Sandbrand.
And he was born, according to the birth certificate I just received, March 28, 1923.
He was two years older, yes.
I was seven days old.
He died-- he passed away at my bris.
Well, during World War I, he was a youngster.
He was too young to serve the Austrian army, which was-- Kolomyja was part of Austria in those days.
And he came from a small town, which was about 20 kilometers away from Kolomyja.
It was called Jablonuw.
J-A-B-L-O-N-O-- or N-U-W.
And he went to visit his father in this village.
And he was walking through the field and found a round object made out of steel with a hook on it.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He figured-- in our living room, in our-- one of the rooms that we lived in at, at that time-- the family, actually, I wasn't there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a kerosene lamp, which was hanging on a chain.
And it needed a counter weight so it could move up and down.
So he brought this object home.
And it was laying there for a few months until before Passover.
He picked it up, painted it up, and hung it up as the counterweight.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Passover night, he came home from shul, from the synagogue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was standing at the table, saying kiddush.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they turned up the kerosene lamp.
And that object heated up.
And it exploded.
Apparently, it was some kind of a bomb.
A shrapnel went through his lungs.
The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] police came, the Polish police.
And instead of sending him to the hospital, they arrested him for manufacturing a bomb.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish population of the town at the time figured it was an anti-Semitic act, because the, the house was lit up and the windows were wide open.
And they thought somebody threw a bomb in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The investigation proved that what he said was true, you know, that he picked up the object in the field.
And two months later, they released him from prison.
He came home a day before my bris.
And that day, he got up to open a barrel of beer.
He had a hemorrhage.
And he died.
I was told that if he would have died a day before, I would have been named after him.
But he died after my naming.
It was a nice size city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- the population, I believe, was about 18,000 or 20,000.
It was a county seat, Kolomyja.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- had-- we had a railroad going right through the middle of town.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were many, many synagogues.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had one beautiful main synagogue.
We had many well-to-do people, Jewish people.
And we also had a large proportion of very, very poor people, Jewish people.
Poor people that were struggling to make a living.
We survived because mother had sisters in the United States.
And they kept her once for a Jewish holiday.
They used to send $10, or $15, or $20 to help.
Their old parents, which they left many, many years before, and they never saw them again.
Well, we had just enough to eat.
Once in a Passover, they used to send us a package of old clothes.
Well, she-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like everyone else in those days, used to buy a piece of cloth, and sell a piece of cloth, and make a few cents on it.
But in later years, she organized a kindergarten type of a, like a, a babysitting service or Hebrew school.
And she had about 15 to 20 children and preschoolers.
And that helped her survive.
While those people worked, yes.
With a lot of those children, yes.
Was it what?
Oh, yes, yeah.
The religious were mainly the, the-- I'm looking for the word-- [LAUGHS] the organizers.
But we also had the [NON-ENGLISH], which organized youth for Palestine, and the shomrim.
And they had many, many Jewish organizations.
They had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are we just discussing the Jewish part of it, or, in general, the whole city?
The Jewish community-- they had-- what do you call it-- where they organized self-- where the richer part of the Jewish community was helping with a hospital, with an old age home.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mind you, when the war started, I was only 14 in 1939.
And as a youngster, I paid no-- I didn't pay too much attention to that.
But in those days, the older people lived with the younger ones until we die-- [LAUGHS] until they passed away, if they had a chance to, to live that long.
And, and that was about it.
Well, grandfather used to get up very early in the morning.
Yes, Grandma and Grandfather-- yes, the two of them, and also an aunt, my mother's sister, her husband and three kids.
We lived all in the same house.
It had four rooms.
My mother and I, and my grandfather, and grandmother, and our brother, my brother, lived on one side of the house.
It was like it had a hallway in between.
On one side, It had a old-fashioned kitchen with a oven on top.
And it had one room where we slept, all slept in that one room.
No, the grandparents slept in the kitchen part.
It was large enough to put a bed in.
Mother and us two boys slept in the other room.
Across the hall in one room was mother's sister, her husband, and three children.

And the front room was rented out to a carpenter who used to produce, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make all kinds of wooden furniture, and also made the Jewish lions-- I just remembered that-- for the synagogue.
You know those that adorned the Torah part-- on top of the Torah.
He was a very skilled carpenter, an artist, I would call him.
And that helped us survive, that rental also helps--
Survive, yes.
My maternal grandparents.
My grandfather's name was Elay Hacker, H-A-C-K-E-R.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his wife's name was Lifsha.
Lifsha.
I don't know how to spell it.
Yes.
My mother sister's name was Pepi.
I would say P-E-P-I.
And she married a man by the name of Steinkohl-- you know, stone and coal.
S-T-E-I-N-K-O-H-L.
Pinchas.
Yes, their son, Mendel, was born in 1922.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] His sister, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Rachel, was born in 1921.
And he had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another sister, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], Rose.
And she was born, I believe, in 1927 or '28.
Lived together in that house.
Not in the house.
Well, they apparently rented a gardens in, in the near vicinity with a little shack like.
And that's where the children used to be entertained, and played with, and whatever was necessary for a set amount of hours.
It wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was like a seven or eight-hour thing.
Every day, yes.
Five days a week.
Not until 1938.
They first installed one bulb in one room.
No.
The water, we had to carry for about a kilometer away.
They had one faucet like in the middle of a town.
And everybody used to come with pails.
And we used to carry the water home.
We had outhouses.
It was a little shack.
And we used to go outside.
And twice a year, a man used to come and empty it out with a barrel and a horse, real modern.
[LAUGHS]
Well--
Well, as I went to school, the area where I went to school was mostly a Jewish area.
We lived like in a isolated ghetto part, mostly poor Jewish people.
And the school was run by the Polish government.
I would say that about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30% or 40% of the students were Polish and Ukrainian.
Actually, most Polish students and Ukrainian students were going to their own parochial, church-sponsored schools in those days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a predominantly Jewish school.
But the curriculum was in Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And most of the teachers, I would say, were anti-Semitic.

Well, we had-- classes began in the morning with a religious-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody had to stand up and pray-- though I kept my mouth shut, I didn't say anything-- in a Catholic-Christian prayer, which I still remember.
And so they were very rough, the way they treated to us as Jews with remarks, you know?
And in general, you could feel the anti-Semitism.
I could feel it as a child.
Yes, I was hit a few times with a long ruler, yelled at, screamed at.
By the teachers.
I had a few encounters there, too.
Well, you would expect children to fight in classes, but to be called continuously dirty Jew, [NON-ENGLISH] which means filthy, full of vermin Jew, you know?
In class many times and as we left school, fights ensued.
On way home.
I was ganged up on by some, some of the students.
I hit back.
And they complained that I hit them, you know?
So we, we got into trouble because of it.
I was suspended.
I was sent home to bring my mother.
How-- and when my mother came, how dare I start a fight and hit, you know, other kids.
[LAUGHS]
It was not too bad.
They taught us how to read and write Polish, mathematics.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was pretty good at it, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the war started.
And we didn't have a chance to go anywhere else.
Except my older brother did go to gymnasium, which was like a high school, I would say.
But you had to pay for it.
Well, I, I went to about seventh grade.
And the war started.
Actually, in '39, I finished the fifth grade.
Then the Russians came in, the Soviet Union.
And I went two grades in their schools.
Right, right.
They took over the public schools, but it was a completely different situation.
You were able to speak Yiddish in class if you wanted to.
No.
You had Jewish teachers that were actually teaching in Yiddish.
Certain subjects, yes.
Yes, yeah.
They were teaching about Sholem Aleichem.
They were teaching about Paris, you know, and different Yiddish subjects.
OK.
Yes, sir.
